name: Hawaiian Kociemba
description: Hawaiian Kociemba is a method inspired by Herbert Kociemba's two-phase computer algorithm, adapted for human solving. The method starts by orienting all edges and forming an 'arrow' pattern on the first face, then completes the solve using a restricted RUL moveset. It is named for its origin in the Hawaiian cubing community. The approach is relatively accessible while achieving a respectable move count.

steps.name: EO Arrow
steps.description: Orient all 12 edges and simultaneously create an 'arrow' shape on the bottom face — the cross center plus one correctly placed edge and its adjacent corner-edge pair. This step establishes the foundation while ensuring all edges are oriented for an efficient finish.
tips: Learn to spot bad edges quickly using the F/B axis rule
The arrow gives a starting point for F2L-like solving
Practice combining EO with arrow formation

steps.name: First Two Layers with RUL
steps.description: Complete the first two layers using only R, U, and L moves (plus their inverses and double turns). Since all edges are oriented, F2L pairs can be built and inserted without F or B moves, similar to ZZ's F2L step.
tips: Very similar to ZZF2L once EO is done
RUL moveset is ergonomic and rotation-free
Plan pair insertions to maintain efficiency

steps.name: Last Layer
steps.description: Solve the last layer. With edges already oriented, the last layer is reduced to OCLL (orient corners) followed by PLL, or can be solved with ZBLL for a single-look finish.
tips: Only 7 OCLL cases since edges are oriented
COLL + EPLL is an efficient intermediate approach

pros: Interesting bridge between beginner and advanced methods
cons: EO arrow step requires practice to plan efficiently
EO recognition can be tricky for beginners
